coredata.dc:title: Flexible polypyrrole/polyvinyl alcohol/titanium dioxide/graphene quantum dots nanocomposite films for energy storage devices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Salman, Muhammad
Aslam, Muhammad
Mustafa, Zeeshan
Ahmad, Muhammad
Shehzad, Asif
coredata.dc:description: Polypyrrole/polyvinyl alcohol (PPy/PVA) impregnated titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) flexible nanocomposite films (PPy/PVA, PPy/PVA/TiO2, PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs) were fabricated using a solution casting approach and characterized via advanced techniques. Structural, morphological, elemental, chemical, and wetting analyses were performed. A minute quantity of TiO2 nanoparticles and GQDs in the PPy/PVA matrix blend induced hydrophobicity, flexibility, and strength. Water contact angle analysis showed moisture resistance in an open environment. The cyclic voltammetry analysis showed pseudocapacitive behaviour. The galvanostatic charge-discharge analysis showed excellent improvement in the specific capacitance (72.6–219.4 F g−1), energy density (1.23–3.73 W h kg−1), power density (0.175–0.350 kW kg−1), and excellent cyclic stability after 5000 cycles, on incorporation of GQDs in PPy/PVA matrix blend. The fabricated flexible PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film might be used in wearable electrode material in energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy storage
Flexible
Graphene quantum dots
Polypyrrole
Titanium dioxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of polypyrrole/titanium dioxide/graphene quantum dots electrode films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for fabricating PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film.
XRD spectra of the PPy/PVA, PPy/PVA/TiO2, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite films.

(a) The SEM micrographs of TiO2 NPs at 200,000 ×, (b) histogram for the size of TiO2 NPs, (c) EDX spectrum of TiO2 NPs, (d) SEM micrograph of PPy/PVA at 1000 ×, (e) EDX spectrum of PPy/PVA, (f) SEM micrograph of PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs at 1000 ×, (g) EDX spectrum of PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs film, and (h) TEM image of PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film.
FTIR patterns of PPy, PPy/PVA, TiO2 NPs, PPy/PVA/TiO2, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite films.
Water contact angle of the PPy/PVA, PPy/PVA/TiO2, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite films.
Representative (a) stress-strain plots, (b) tensile strength, Young's modulus, and elongation at break (%) of the PPy/PVA, PPy/PVA/TiO2, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite films.
CV curves at scanning rates of 10, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 mV/s for (a) PPy/PVA, (b) PPy/PVA/TiO2, and (c) PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs films.
GCD curves of (a) PPy/PVA, (b) PPy/PVA/TiO2, (c) PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs films at 1, 1.5, and 2 A g−1.

(a) Specific capacitance versus current density (1, 1.5, and 2 A g−1) for the fabricated nanocomposite films, and (b) specific capacitance retention (%) versus cycle number for PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs at 1 A g−1.
Nyquist plot after performing CV and GCD analysis for PPy/PVA and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposites.
Comparison of calculated parameters for the PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs as electrode material with earlier PPy-related work.

head.ce:title: Flexible polypyrrole/polyvinyl alcohol/titanium dioxide/graphene quantum dots nanocomposite films for energy storage devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Sciences, National Textile University, Faisalabad 37610, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Sciences, National Textile University, Faisalabad, 37610, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering and Technology, National Textile University, Faisalabad 37610, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering and Technology, National Textile University, Faisalabad, 37610, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polypyrrole/polyvinyl alcohol (PPy/PVA) impregnated titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) flexible nanocomposite films (PPy/PVA, PPy/PVA/TiO2, PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs) were fabricated using a solution casting approach and characterized via advanced techniques. Structural, morphological, elemental, chemical, and wetting analyses were performed. A minute quantity of TiO2 nanoparticles and GQDs in the PPy/PVA matrix blend induced hydrophobicity, flexibility, and strength. Water contact angle analysis showed moisture resistance in an open environment. The cyclic voltammetry analysis showed pseudocapacitive behaviour. The galvanostatic charge-discharge analysis showed excellent improvement in the specific capacitance (72.6–219.4 F g−1), energy density (1.23–3.73 W h kg−1), power density (0.175–0.350 kW kg−1), and excellent cyclic stability after 5000 cycles, on incorporation of GQDs in PPy/PVA matrix blend. The fabricated flexible PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film might be used in wearable electrode material in energy storage devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Flexible electrode materials are leading the way in providing the electrochemical performance needed for advancing next-generation energy storage systems. Polymer nanocomposites, formed by integrating nanostructures into the polymer blends, exhibit significantly enhanced properties compared to their micro- and macro-scale counterparts [1,2]. Organic-inorganic composites combine the functional properties of the organic components with the structural stability and robustness of the inorganic components. This synergy allows them to exhibit enhanced performance, making them suitable for various advanced applications [3,4]. Recent studies show that polypyrrole (PPy) is a key material because of its high electrical conductivity, reversible doping/dedoping behaviour, straightforward synthesis process, and environmental adaptability. These attributes make PPy suitable for batteries, capacitors, and supercapacitors [5-7]. However, the performance of PPy decreases with high mass loading and larger electrode sizes because of restricted ion diffusion and a decline in accessible surface area. To address this, incorporating composite materials has enhanced its electrochemical efficiency. Renewable biopolymers such as cellulose, starch, chitosan, and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) have been widely utilized as host polymers. PVA is considered due to the high density of reactive functional groups, which facilitate compatibility with conducting salts and plasticizers [8]. PVA offers several advantages, including ease of preparation and excellent charge storage capabilities, making it a strong candidate for use in flexible electrochemical devices [9].

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) exhibits chemical stability, excellent electrochemical properties, affordability, and low toxicity [10]. Integrating inorganic TiO2 nano-powder into organic polymer blends significantly enhances energy storage devices' capacitance, stability, and electrochemical performance. Al-hakimi et al. [11] fabricated PPy/TiO2 nanocomposite films with different concentrations of inorganic TiO2 nanoparticles for energy storage systems. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess a large aspect ratio and enhanced surface grafting capabilities due to their π-π conjugate bonding structure. Their unique functional groups contribute to the presence of a nonzero band gap, making them distinctive from traditional graphene [12]. GQDs are particularly valuable as conductive additives in the electrodes of energy storage devices [13].

PPy-based nanocomposites have been widely studied. Ruthes et al. [14] fabricated flexible hydrogel-based electrodes to store charges. Electro-polymerization of PPy nanotubes was done in the presence of graphite thread electrode materials utilizing methyl orange in an acidic moderator. The synthesized electrode exhibited a specific capacitance of 66 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, power density of 19.9 W kg−1, and energy density of 3 W h kg−1. Sun et al. [15] developed PPy/carboxylate cellulose nanocrystal films to enhance capacitive behaviour and cyclic stability. After the addition of sodium perchlorate and potassium chloride, they observed enhancements in the energy density (5.2 W h kg−1), current density (2 A g−1), power density (25.4 W kg−1), and specific capacitance (125 F g−1). Bonastre et al. [16] prepared flexible, low-cost, eco-friendly jute-based conducting supercapacitor fabric utilizing reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and conducting PPy contents. The rGO-PPy-jute supercapacitor material exhibited a capacitance of 117 F g−1. Wilczewska et al. [17] examined the effect of GQDs theoretically and experimentally on the electrochemical behaviour of PPy nanoparticles. The PPy/GQDs material was synthesized using the chemical polymerization of pyrrole. The GQDs considerably changed the energy states at the interface of PPy/GQDs, enhancing charge transportation reactions in the composite faster than PPy film and improving electrochemical performance.

Herein, flexible, lightweight PPy/PVA, PPy/PVA/TiO2, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode films were synthesized using a solution casting technique. The objective of the present work was to investigate the effect of incorporating TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) and GQDs on structural, elemental, wetting, mechanical, and electrochemical properties of the PPy/PVA matrix blend using advanced characterization techniques. In this study, a conducting polymer PPy provides conductivity and pseudo capacitance, PVA adds mechanical flexibility and electrolyte support, TiO2 and GQDs are the primary source of charge storage, ensure structural stability and enhance ion storage, and boost the conductivity and surface area for efficient charge storage. Electrochemical results of the present work showed enhancements in specific capacitance (72.6–219.4 F g−1), energy density (1.23–3.73 W h kg−1), power density (0.175–0.350 kW kg−1), and cyclic stability owing to the addition of TiO2/GQDs in the PPy/PVA matrix blend. Enhancements were also observed in Young's modulus (220–660 MPa), tensile strength (30.5–33.9 MPa), and elasticity. The novelty of the fabricated electrode films might be ascertained by comparing the results of the present work with the earlier work [14–17]. The fabricated lightweight flexible PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film might be used for wearable energy storage systems.

Polypyrrole (conductivity 10 to 50 S cm−1, pressed pellets), titanium (IV) oxide nanoparticles (TEM size 21 nm), graphene quantum dots (1 mg/mL in H2O, blue luminescent), and N·N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were got from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Polyvinyl alcohol (powder form, degree of polymerization 1500) was obtained from Reagents Duksan, Korea. The Polypyrrole pellets and other chemicals were utilized as received without any modifications or further purification. Distilled water was made in the lab.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of polypyrrole/titanium dioxide/graphene quantum dots electrode films
ce:section.ce:para: PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode films were fabricated via the solution casting technique. PVA (2 g) in powder form was liquified in 50 mL distilled water using magnetic stirring at 60 °C for 24 h. The PPy pellets (200 mg) were dissolved in 30 mL dimethylformamide solution at 80 °C for 24 h on a hot plate while stirring. The PPy solution was pipetted in the PVA solution and stirred vigorously at 30 °C for 48 h. The TiO2 NPs (14.6 wt% of PPy) were introduced in the PPy/PVA blend and stirred overnight at 30 °C. The GQDs (4 wt% of PPy) were added dropwise using a micropipette in the PPy/PVA/TiO2 dispersion and stirring at 30 °C for 48 h. The dispersion was poured cautiously onto a Petri dish and dried in a vacuum oven at 30 °C. Finally, the dried electrode film was detached from the Petri dish for subsequent characterizations and investigations. The fabrication procedural steps of the PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film are shown in Fig. 1 . In this study, three-electrode films were fabricated in all, namely, PPy/PVA, PPy/PVA/TiO2 and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs, and the average thickness was recorded 200 μm, employing a micrometer.

The phases of the fabricated electrode films were identified by employing an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker AXS D8 Advance, Germany) in the measuring range of 10° to 80° using CuKα radiations. FEI Nova Nano SEM 450 coupled with EDX was engaged for the surface morphology and elemental analysis. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100F) examined microstructures. To determine chemical interactions, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Bruker, Tensor 27) was employed in the wavenumbers of 600 to 4000 cm−1. Attention® Theta lite optical tensiometer was employed to measure the water contact angles. Mechanical parameters of the fabricated electrode films (strip size 10 × 0.5 cm2) were determined using a universal testing machine (Instron 4302, 5 kN load cell, UK). A Zahner Zennium CIMPS electrochemical workstation was employed to analyze the fabricated electrode’ galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) spectra. Three electrodes (fabricated film as working electrode, platinum plate as counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as reference electrode) were considered to the study the electrochemical properties. The fabricated films were cut into 1 × 1 cm2 size. A 3-M potassium hydroxide solution was used for an electrolyte. These three electrodes were placed inside the beaker. The CV spectra were gathered at a scan rate of 10, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 mV/s and a potential difference of 0 to 0.4 V. The GCD scanning was done at a current density of 1, 1.5, and 2 A g−1 and a potential difference of 0 to 0.4 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed over a frequency range 105 to 0.1 Hz at open circuit potential using an AC perturbation of 5 mV.

The changes in the crystalline phase of the material might affect its physical and chemical behaviour. The literature shows that PVA produces a unique peak at the 2θ position of 19.4° (101) owing to its semicrystalline nature, with an interplanar distance of 0.44 nm [18,19]. The XRD pattern of PPy indicates no significant peak due to its amorphous nature [20]. The diffraction patterns of the fabricated electrode films are shown in Fig. 2 . The PPy/PVA matrix blend pattern indicates a reflection peak at the 2θ position of 19.7° (101) and an interplanar distance of 0.38 nm. Compared to the PVA peak, there is a slight change in the peak position and the interplanar distance, owing to hydrogen bonding between nitrogen groups of PPy and OH- groups of PVA [21]. Hydrogen bonding might alter the ordered structure, the π-conjugation, and its conductivity. The crystallinity (%) of the PPy/PVA blend was calculated as 81 %, following the procedure of our previous work [22]. In the PPy/PVA/TiO2 pattern, a reflection peak appeared at the 2θ position of 19.7° (101) due to PVA. Other low-intensity peaks were also observed due to TiO2 NPs at 2θ positions of 25.28° (101), 37.82° (004), 47.82° (200), and 54.18° (106), with an interplanar distance of 0.26 nm (101) and crystallite size of 49.39 nm [23]. The low-intensity peaks were mixed with noise peaks due to the minimal amount of TiO2 NPs in the PPy/PVA blend. The peak positions of TiO2 NPs remained the same in both the samples of PPy/PVA/TiO2 and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs, indicating no change in the phase and structure of the TiO2 NPs. This sample's crystallinity (%) decreased slightly to 80 % after incorporating TiO2 NPs due to introducing defects and an amorphous phase into the PPy/PVA matrix blend. A prominent reflection peak of GQDs was observed at 2θ position 25.45° corresponding to the (002) plane in the PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs pattern [24]. The crystallite size of GQDs in this sample was measured as 13 nm using Scherrer's formula and an interplanar distance of 0.11 nm [25]. The crystallinity (%) of PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs film was further decreased to 71 % after incorporating GQDs due to the increasing number of defects in the PPy/PVA/TiO2 [26]. A reduction in the peak intensity at the 2θ position of 19.71° indicated that the active functional groups of GQDs were arranged in a disordered way. The collaboration between nanofillers and the PPy/PVA matrix blend produced defects and distortions. Lowering crystallinity provides a reasonable path for the transmission of charge carriers [27,28]. The lowering of crystallinity (%) is also indicated by decreasing d-spacing after adding TiO2 NPs and GQDs. The decreasing trend of crystallinity (%) predicts the decreasing brittleness in the samples after nanofillers' incorporation. These results indicated that TiO2 NPs and GQDs were comprehensively dispersed into the PPy/PVA matrix blend, and the electrode films were successfully fabricated.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) assessment shows nanofillers' morphological structure and dispersion level within the electrode films. Fig. 3 illustrates the micrographs and EDX spectra of the TiO2 NPs, PPy/PVA, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite films. Fig. 3a shows a micrograph of TiO2 NPs indicating their spherical shape with some agglomeration aggregation due to weak inter-particle forces causing particle adhesion [29]. Fig. 3b shows a Gaussian-fitted histogram of TiO2 NPs. The elemental composition of the fabricated electrode films was investigated using an EDX detector. Fig. 3c shows the EDX spectrum of TiO2 NPs, indicating the presence of elements titanium (49.18 at.%), oxygen (45.27 at.%), and carbon (5.55 at.%). A PPy/PVA sample micrograph indicates roughness and surface cracks, shown in Fig. 3d. Strong interconnectivity between PPy and PVA controls the elongation and mobility of polymer chains. The PPy/PVA micrograph displays less elongation behaviour due to surface roughness and cracks [30]. The EDX spectrum of the PPy/PVA film revealed carbon (59.16 at.%) and oxygen (40.84 at.%) elements, shown in Fig. 3e. Adding TiO2 NPs and GQDs in the PPy/PVA blend furnish flatness, levelness, and better structural integrity with no phase separation, as shown in Fig. 3f, revealing the formation of a homogeneous film. It also confirms the enhancement in the amorphous phase because of the collaboration of TiO2 NPs, GQDs, and polymeric chains. Notably, the dispersion of TiO2 NPs and GQDs appears uniform and without any evident aggregation. These observations highlight the excellent compatibility among TiO2 NPs, GQDs, and the polymer blend. SEM analysis suggests a potential enhancement in the flexibility and capacitive behaviour of the prepared nanocomposite films. The PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film displays surface roughness and promotes linkage. Moving to the EDX spectrum of the PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite film, all aspects comprising TiO2, GQDs, PVA, and PPy were depicted in Fig. 3g. No extra element was observed, affirming the phase purity of the synthesized electrode films.

The PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film was subjected to a TEM scanning to investigate the microstructures, as shown in Fig. 3h. It depicts well-dispersed and absorbed GQDs in the polymer blend without any apparent agglomeration due to the low content of GQDs. It is observed that the size distribution of the GQDs is considerably homogeneous. The average diameter of GQDs was observed as 3.81 nm. The image demonstrates that GQDs have a noticeable lattice structure and fringe spacing of 0.11 nm [31]. Following XRD findings, the lattice plane distance of 0.26 nm was also observed, indicating the presence of TiO2 NPs.

FTIR analysis investigated the intermolecular interactions between the matrix blend and the incorporated nanofillers. Fig. 4 shows the FTIR patterns of pure PPy, PPy/PVA, TiO2 NPs, PPy/PVA/TiO2, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs films. The spectrum of PPy showed peaks at 2925 and 1580 cm−1 related to CH stretching and NH bending, respectively. The PPy/PVA matrix blend exhibited a significant characteristic peak at 3290 cm−1, related to hydroxyl symmetric stretching. The NH and CC bending vibrations were found at 1327 and 1420 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 910 and 1660 cm−1 were observed due to the CN stretching and CH wagging, respectively [32]. Ti-OH stretching and TiO bending were found in the pattern of TiO2 NPs at 1702 and 654 cm-1, respectively [33]. Due to hydroxyl stretching, the PPy/PVA/TiO2 nanocomposite also showed a broad peak at 3290 cm-1. The PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs pattern represented distinctive peaks due to the interaction of PPy, PVA, TiO2, and GQDs. The peak at 1669 cm−1 was observed due to PPy/PVA and TiO2 interaction. The peak at 1050 cm−1 was found due to the CO stretching, the peak at 1243 cm−1 represented the PPy ring's CN stretching, and the peak at 1428 cm−1 was correlated to the hydroxyl stretching of PVA. The results showed that the PPy/PVA blend interacted with TiO2 NPs. The 850 and 1125 cm−1 peak shifting indicated the interaction between PPy/PVA/TiO2 and GQDs [34]. The interactions of PPy/PVA, TiO2 NPs, and GQDs indicated the successful formulation of the PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film.

The water contact angle of the fabricated electrode films is shown in Fig. 5 . Increase in water contact angle increases hydrophobicity character, which is a desired property for the fabricated films. The water contact angles of 31.17°, 63.51°, and 80.37° were observed for PPy/PVA, PPy/PVA/TiO2, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrodes, respectively. Adding TiO2 NPs and GQDs enhanced the hydrophobicity of the fabricated films [35]. GQDs possess a highly hydrophobic surface due to the availability of the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. When embedded into the PPy/PVA matrix blend, the hydrophobic surfaces of GQDs affected the overall hydrophobic character of the nanocomposite film. Incorporating TiO2 and GQDs reduced the surface energy of the PPy/PVA matrix blend, which decreased the material's affinity for water molecules [36,37]. Li et al. [36] concluded that GQDs induce anticorrosive hydrophobic character in polyphenylene sulfide. The TiO2/GQDs nanofillers promoted closer packing of the matrix blend chains in nanocomposite film, lessening water molecule penetration. With increased hydrophobicity, the film shows more moisture resistance and stability in the open environment. It is an essential property for any film for energy storage applications to avoid environmental degradation. The sample's hydrophobic nature can improve resistance to degradation in aqueous environment. The nanoscale features provide a large active surface area, facilitating efficient electron transfer and charge storage. Hydrophobicity can help minimize unwanted interaction with the electrolyte, improving efficiency and cycle life. Thus, PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film might last longer than PPy/PVA and PPy/PVA/TiO2 films, enhancing its commercial viability.

Fig. 6 showed significant enhancements in the mechanical properties of the PPy/PVA matrix blend after adding TiO2 NPs and GQDs. The highest value of stress that can withstand a material is tensile strength, whereas Young's modulus measures the material's resistance to stretching. The tensile strength of the PPy/PVA blend was measured as 30.5 MPa, slightly increased to 30.9 MPa, then further increased to 33.9 MPa after incorporation of TiO2 NPs and GQDs, respectively. The Young's modulus of PPy/PVA blend was measured as 220 MPa, increased to 380 MPa, and then 660 MPa after incorporating TiO2 NPs and GQDs, respectively. The elongation at break (%) of the PPy/PVA blend increased from 134.3 % to 202.6 % and then decreased to 190.6 %. The decreasing trend of crystallinity (%) observed in the XRD analysis indicated a decrease in the brittleness of the fabricated samples. The calculated parametric values of the fabricated films agreed with the literature [38–40]. The improvement in Young's modulus and tensile strength might be because of the greater surface area of the nanofillers, their effective dispersion, and interfacial linkage among TiO2 NPs, GQDs, and PVA/PPy blend chains [41]. The combined influence of GQDs and TiO2 NPs on the matrix blend resulted in synergistic effects, where the two nanofillers work together to enhance the properties of the nanocomposites. The unique properties of each component can complement and amplify the impact of the other, leading to a more significant improvement in Young's modulus.

The CV and GCD patterns of the fabricated electrodes were analyzed for the electrochemical properties' enhancements. The optimal value of TiO2 concentration in the PPy/PVA/TiO2 electrode has been used in this study to achieve maximum performance as an electrode material. In literature, PPy nanocomposite film exhibited high specific capacitance when a TiO2 concentration of 14.6 wt% of PPy was incorporated [42]. The exact concentration of TiO2 (14.6 wt% of PPy) was used in this work. The synthesized slurry (80 wt% of the fabricated material +10 wt% binder +10 wt% carbon black) was deposited as an electrode made of nickel foam of size (1 cm2). The CV curves of the fabricated electrodes were plotted at scanning rates of 10, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 mV s-1, using a potential window of 0–0.4 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and shown in Fig. 7 . Notably, the presence of redox peaks indicates good reversible stability and pseudocapacitive performance. During the electrochemical process, redox peaks were recognized owing to the depreciation of the residual oxygen functional groups of the fabricated films. Redox peaks at different scan rates exhibited a faradic charging process. The increased area under the curve was observed with incrementing scanning rate, a common trend in the literature [43]. Current density and surface area affected the broadening of the redox peaks. The CV analysis showed that the PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs can oxidize at a lower potential with a much higher current value. This increase in the peak current might be attributed to enhanced electrochemical activity induced by the TiO2 NPs and the GQDs in the electrode [44]. The GQDs have electrocatalytic properties that can lower the potential required for oxidation, resulting in a shift in the oxidation peak. The improved electrochemical performance of the fabricated electrodes could be recognized due to the higher surface area provided by the nanofillers, presenting enhanced binding sites for the interaction [45].

Fig. 8 illustrates the GCD spectra of the fabricated electrode films at current densities of 1, 1.5, and 2 A g−1. The spectra indicate the charging and discharging potential of the fabricated electrode films. The GCD profiles are comparable to the CV spectra, indicating that the substances contributing to the slope are pseudocapacitive. On incrementing the current density, the discharge time duration reduces, possibly due to a significant collision rate and enhancement in charge transport excitation. A slower discharging rate was observed for the PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film compared to PPy/PVA and PPy/PVA/TiO2, suggesting its higher specific capacitance [17]. So, PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film might have high energy capacity due to fast electronic flow on the PPy/PVA surface by TiO2 NPs and GQDs.

The GCD curves were used to measure the specific capacitance C
s of the fabricated films (as electrode material) using an equation [46]:
(1)
C s
=
I × ∆ t
m × ∆ V

I, m, ΔV, and Δt represent the applied current, active mass, potential window, and discharge time. Fig. 9a shows the specific capacitance of PPy/PVA, PPy/PVA/TiO2, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrodes at current densities of 1, 1.5, and 2 A g−1. Higher specific capacitance was noted at the current density of 1 A g−1 compared to 1.5 and 2 A g−1. The C
s of the PPy/PVA and PPy/PVA/TiO2 films at 1 A g−1 were calculated as 72.6 F g−1 and 161.4 F g−1, respectively. The enhancement in Cs was due to the addition of TiO2 NPs. Furthermore, when GQDs (4 wt% of PPy) were incorporated into the PPy/PVA/TiO2, the Cs were further enhanced to 219.4 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 owing to the high surface area. This enhancement in Cs was consistent with the literature [47]. The more extended area under the current potential curve correlates with higher Cs in CV curves, highlighting the importance of optimizing electrode materials for improved electrochemical performance. Based on these observations, the PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film emerged as the optimal choice among all the fabricated electrode films, demonstrating the highest specific capacitance. The specific capacitance retention for PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs film was observed to be smaller than the PPy/PVA and PPy/PVA/TiO2 electrode films with the increasing current density. The decline in specific capacitance retention with the rise in current density is mainly attributed to ion diffusion limitation, heightened internal resistance and ohmic losses, kinetic constraints of faradaic reactions, and possible structural degradation of the electrode material. These factors collectively diminish the efficiency and effectiveness of charge storage at a more significant current density, ending in lower observed capacitance retention values. Fig. 9b shows the cyclic stability of the fabricated PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film at 1 A g−1 using GCD cycles. This electrode has shown maximum specific capacitance retention of 88 % after 5000 cycles. The exceptional cyclic stability might be due to the interconnected structures of TiO2 and GQDs with the PPy/PVA blend.

The energy density E d and power density P d were measured from the GCD data using the equations [48]:
(2)
E d
=
1 2
C s
ΔV
2
(3)
P d
=
E d
t

The higher energy density value is the fundamental requirement for flexible energy storage devices. The energy density for PPy/PVA blend, PPy/PVA/TiO2, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrodes at 1 A g−1 was calculated as 1.23, 2.75, and 3.73 W h kg−1, respectively. These values are per the literature [49]. The 3.73 W h kg−1 energy density suggests an appropriate balance for the devices needing prolonged usage without excessive weight. The power density for PPy/PVA blend, PPy/PVA/TiO2, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrodes at 1 A g−1 was calculated as 0.175, 0.262, and 0.350 kW kg−1. The higher power density might enable the PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode to effectively handle rapid charge/discharge cycles. It might provide sufficient energy storage and the capability to deliver power quickly, making it suitable for various wearable and portable devices requiring flexibility and efficiency. The fabricated film can provide a reliable energy supply and quick response for medical monitoring or therapeutic devices. Table 1 shows the superior results of the fabricated PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs film compared to other studies' PPy-based electrode materials.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy is a crucial technique for evaluating the fundamental performance of capacitive electrodes and the transfer of electrons and ions on the surfaces. EIS measurements were performed over a frequency range 105 to 0.1 Hz at open circuit potential using an AC perturbation of 5 mV. The Nyquist plot for PPy/PVA and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite was analyzed and fitted using an appropriate equivalent circuit model. In this model, parameters such as solution resistance (R s), pseudo-capacitance (C ps), charge transfer resistance (R ct), double-layer capacitance (C dl), and Warburg impedance related to ion diffusion (Z W) were included. The Nyquist plot shown in Fig. 10 can be divided into high-frequency and low-frequency regions. In the high-frequency region, no semicircle was observed for any of the electrodes [53]. For PPy/PVA, the Nyquist plot shows a slopping line in the low-frequency region. This behaviour arises due to the high charge density, which interferes with ion transfer resistance. The 45° slope in the low-frequency region corresponds to Warburg resistance. A high Warburg resistance increases ion diffusion path length and hinders ion movement at low frequencies [54]. For PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite, a smaller equivalent series resistance (ESR) was observed. It might be attributed to the strong interaction between the polymer blend and nanofillers. The TiO2 NPs and GQDs network enhanced the accessibility of electrolyte ions to the electrode surface and reduced the ion diffusion path length, facilitating efficient mass transfer. The behaviour is supported using the PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite as a pseudo-capacitor electrode material.

The PPy/PVA, PPy/PVA/TiO2, and PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs nanocomposite electrode films were prepared successfully using a solution casting method. The XRD and SEM investigations confirmed the good dispersion of TiO2 NPs and GQDs in the PPy/PVA matrix blend. The elemental purity was confirmed by EDX analysis. FTIR investigations indicated the effective formulation of the electrode films. The water contact angle analysis showed that due to the inclusion of the TiO2 NPs and GQDs, the hydrophobicity of the electrode film enhanced (31.17° − 80.37°). The fabricated electrode film might show more moisture resistance and stability in the open environment. It is an essential property of any electrode material for energy storage applications to avoid environmental degradation. The tensile testing showed enhancement in Young's modulus (220–660 MPa), tensile strength (30.5 to 33.9 MPa), and percentage elongation at break (134.3–202.6 %) after incorporation of the nanofillers. The fabricated electrode films are stronger and more flexible. The improvement in the mechanical properties is attributed to the effective dispersion of nanofillers, their large aspect ratio, and the excellent interconnection of TiO2 NPs and GQDs with the PVA, and PPy chains. The plotted CV patterns of the fabricated electrodes at scanning rates of 10, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 mV s-1, and a potential window of 0–0.4 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) depicted the existing redox peaks, indicated good reversible stability and pseudocapacitive performance. The GCD spectra were used to calculate specific capacitance and specific capacitance retention at current densities of 1, 1.5, and 2 A g−1. The PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film showed a much-delayed discharge time, indicating enhanced specific capacitance. The cyclic stability, power density, and energy density were also measured using the GCD spectra. The electrochemical analysis showed significant improvement in the specific capacitance (72.6–219.4 F g−1), energy density (1.23–3.73 W h kg−1), and power density (0.175–0.350 kW kg−1) at the current density of 1 A g−1, due to the addition of minute quantity of GQDs (4 wt% of PPy) and TiO2 NPs (14.6 wt% of PPy) into PPy/PVA matrix blend. The EIS analysis shows the pseudo-capacitor behaviour of the fabricated electrode. It is concluded that the fabricated lightweight, long-lasting PPy/PVA/TiO2/GQDs electrode film might be used for flexible and wearable energy storage devices.

Muhammad Salman: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Muhammad Aslam: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Zeeshan Mustafa: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Muhammad Ahmad: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Asif Shehzad: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
